insight: Gauss Cannon's appeal lies in its convenience rather than raw efficiency. It provides on-demand attack whenever the marginal cost of 1 defense exceeds the cost of the charge, requiring no long-term commitment unlike larger attackers.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion about whether Gauss Cannon needs a buff, with bky arguing convenience is as important as efficiency in evaluating units.

insight: Blindly streaming Gauss Cannon charges each turn is suboptimal. The correct play is to make a decision each turn based on the defender's convenience, only buying charges when spending on defense would cost more than the charge itself.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky explaining the correct use of Gauss Cannon in the context of a design discussion about unit efficiency.

insight: Streaming two Gauss Cannon charges per turn costs roughly the equivalent of an Omega Splitter, making mass-streaming very inefficient compared to larger attackers of similar output.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky quantifying the inefficiency of mass-streaming Gauss Cannon charges during a balance discussion.

insight: Nitrocybe is less convenient than Gauss Cannon as an attacker because it is not available on demand, but compensates by being able to block and by allowing faster mass production.
author: .bky_1556
context: Comparative discussion of small attacker units in the context of evaluating convenience vs. efficiency trade-offs.

insight: Cost-efficiency in Prismata is balanced around unit size: larger units receive a bulk discount compared to smaller units, so comparing a small unit's efficiency directly to a large unit's efficiency is misleading.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky explaining why Gauss Cannon's apparent inefficiency is partly a function of its small size relative to comparison units like Immolite.

insight: Shadowfang has very high asymptotic efficiency but much lower practical efficiency, because its tech-rich cost means switching away from it mid-game usually wastes accumulated tech, making it harder to fit into builds than its efficiency numbers suggest.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of how convenience and build-flexibility affect the practical value of high-tech-cost units like Shadowfang versus on-board tech sinks.

insight: Units with on-board tech sinks (abilities that consume tech already in play) partially sidestep the main downside of tech-rich purchase costs, because surplus tech can be spent rather than wasted when pivoting strategies.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky explaining why a hypothetical 5RR unit with a click-to-attack ability avoids the usual inefficiency of tech-heavy costs, contrasting with Shadowfang.

insight: Grimbotch combined with Tarsier produces an attack pattern of 1, 2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1... whereas Tantalum Ray produces 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 1..., giving Tantalum Ray an attack profile that feels similar to red units despite being a different color.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky posting a standalone observation about the attack timing profiles of Grimbotch+Tarsier versus Tantalum Ray.

insight: Militia is considered most effective when bought as the first attacker, as its click ability to snipe an Engineer provides strong early-game finesse; buying it later when defense is already difficult makes the click less useful.
author: iminabearsuit
context: Discussion about whether Militia's cost should be changed; multiple players converged on the view that its current state is well-suited to teaching exploit mechanics.

insight: In Prismata, 'poke' refers to building 1 attack to force the opponent to construct a defender, while continuing to build economy rather than committing to a full attack.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky defining 'poke' in the context of a design discussion about Steelsplitter-synergy units.

insight: The first Blastforge's cost can be partially amortized across the absorb it enables; subsequent Blastforges do not receive this discount, making the marginal cost of a second or third Blastforge higher than the first.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky explaining Blastforge cost accounting in the context of evaluating a hypothetical 7-gold Blastforge variant.

insight: To use a 'golden armor' (build-time) click ability on attackers as a defensive measure against breach, you would need to convert ALL your attackers simultaneously, leaving zero attack on the board for two turns and giving the opponent free turns to destroy your production buildings.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky explaining why a hypothetical click ability granting build-time to Tarsiers as breach protection is effectively useless in practice.

insight: Gauss Cannon's closest efficiency comparators are Pixie (similarly costed for its size) and Immolite (approximately 1.7x the bulk of Gauss Cannon), not larger attackers; comparing it to those larger units overstates its inefficiency.
author: .bky_1556
context: bky countering the claim that Gauss Cannon needs a buff by explaining its correct efficiency comparators.
